title: How much does central heating cost per hour?
description: What UK central heating really costs per hour in 2026: typical gas boiler figures, warm-up versus steady running, and how to measure your own home.
blurb: Typical per-hour costs for gas central heating, why the first hour costs the most, and how to measure yours.
lede: Stand by the thermostat on a cold evening and the question writes itself: if I press this, what does the next hour actually cost me? Most answers online either quote your boiler's maximum rating, which wildly overstates the cost, or give a single national average that tells you nothing about your house. The honest answer is a range, and once you understand what drives it you can pin down your own figure to within a few pence using nothing more than your gas meter and a kettle's worth of patience.
short_answer: For a typical gas-heated three-bed home, central heating costs roughly 25p to 70p per hour at the July to September 2026 price cap rate of 7.33p per kWh of gas. The first hour from cold is the expensive one, around 60p to 75p, because the boiler runs hard to warm the fabric of the house. Once the home is up to temperature, holding it there usually costs 20p to 40p per hour. Small flats sit well below these figures and large detached homes above them.

sections.h2: Where the per-hour cost actually comes from
sections.html:
There is no fixed hourly price for central heating. The cost of any given hour is simply the gas your boiler burns in that hour, in kilowatt hours, multiplied by your unit rate. At the current capped rate that is 7.33p for every kWh of gas. So the whole question collapses into one number: how many kWh does your boiler get through in an hour?

The figure on your boiler's badge is no help here. A 24 kW or 30 kW rating is the maximum output, sized so the boiler can heat your radiators and your hot water on the coldest day of the year. Modern boilers modulate, which means they turn their flame down to match demand, and once your rooms are warm the thermostat switches the burner off entirely for long stretches. A 24 kW boiler running flat out for a full hour would burn through about £1.76 of gas, but outside of the first minutes of a deep winter warm-up, no ordinary house ever sustains that.

In practice the gas your heating uses in an hour is set by three things:

  • How cold it is outside. Your boiler ultimately replaces the heat your house leaks, and a house leaks heat faster when the gap between inside and outside temperature is bigger.
  • How well insulated the house is. A leaky solid-wall Victorian terrace can need twice the gas per hour of a well-insulated newer build of the same size.
  • Whether you are warming up or holding steady. Raising the whole house by several degrees takes far more energy than keeping it where it already is.

One housekeeping note: the per-hour figures on this page cover gas units only. The 29.04p daily gas standing charge applies whether you heat for ten hours or none, so it does not change the marginal cost of an extra hour.

sections.h2: Typical cost per hour by home type
sections.html:
The table below shows realistic gas consumption per hour for common situations, with the cost at the current capped unit rate. Treat the ranges as brackets: a well-insulated home on a mild day sits at the bottom, a draughty one in a cold snap at the top.

Home and situationGas used per hourCost per hour
1 to 2 bed flat, holding temperature, mild day1.5 to 2.5 kWh11p to 18p
1 to 2 bed flat, holding temperature, cold day2.5 to 4 kWh18p to 29p
3 bed semi, holding temperature3 to 5 kWh22p to 37p
3 bed semi, first hour warming up from cold8 to 10 kWh59p to 73p
4 to 5 bed detached, holding temperature, cold day6 to 9 kWh44p to 66p
4 to 5 bed detached, first hour warming up from cold12 to 15 kWh88p to £1.10

At 7.33p per kWh of gas, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Figures assume a modern condensing boiler; older or poorly insulated homes sit at the top of each range or above it.

To turn these into a daily figure, a three-bed semi running its heating for eight hours on a cold day might burn one expensive warm-up hour plus seven cheaper steady hours, landing somewhere around £2.50 to £3.50 for the day. Over a full winter that is where the big annual numbers come from, and our guide to the cost of heating a house for a year works through the season-long picture.

sections.h2: Why the first hour costs the most
sections.html:
When you fire up the heating in a cold house, the boiler is not just warming the air. It is pouring heat into radiators, pipework, walls, floors and furniture, all of which soak up energy before the room feels warm. During this phase the boiler runs at or near full output, so the first hour can easily use two to three times the gas of any later hour.

Once the house reaches the target on your thermostat, everything changes. The burner now only needs to top up what the house loses, so it cycles on and off or modulates right down. Your average consumption drops to match the heat loss of the building, which on a typical cold day is a few kilowatts, not twenty.

This is also why the old argument about leaving the heating on low all day refuses to die. Steady hours look cheap next to that first hour, so it feels like constant heating should win. It usually does not, because every extra hour the house spends warm is an extra hour of heat leaking out that you pay to replace. We unpick the full argument, with the numbers, in is it cheaper to leave the heating on all day?

sections.h2: Gas central heating vs electric heaters vs heat pumps, per hour
sections.html:
Per unit of energy, electricity costs about 3.6 times as much as gas at current capped rates, 26.11p against 7.33p per kWh. That gap dominates any per-hour comparison.

Say your living room and hallway need 5 kWh of heat delivered over an hour on a cold evening. Here is what that hour costs by method:

  • Gas central heating (condensing boiler, around 85% efficient in real use): roughly 5.9 kWh of gas, about 43p.
  • Electric heaters (fan, oil-filled or panel, all effectively 100% efficient): 5 kWh of electricity, about £1.31.
  • Air source heat pump (delivering 3.5 units of heat per unit of electricity): about 1.4 kWh of electricity, roughly 37p.

Two practical conclusions follow. First, heating the whole house electrically with plug-in heaters costs around three times what gas does for the same warmth, so they only make sense for short bursts in one small room while the rest of the house stays cold. Second, a well-installed heat pump already undercuts gas per unit of heat at standard rates, and does so by a wide margin on a heat pump or off-peak tariff. For a single 2 kW plug-in heater, the running cost is simple either way: 2 kWh of electricity per hour, or about 52p.

sections.h2: How to measure your own cost per hour
sections.html:
Your house is not an average, so measure it. You need one evening and either a smart meter display or your gas meter.

With a smart meter in-home display

Wait for a settled hour when nothing else gas-fired is running, so no hot water reheating and no gas hob. Note the day's gas figure, run the heating normally for exactly one hour, then note it again. The difference is your real cost per hour for that outdoor temperature. Do it once during warm-up and once while the house is holding temperature and you will have both ends of your personal range.

With the gas meter itself

Take a meter reading before and after the hour. A metric meter records cubic metres, and each cubic metre of gas contains about 11.2 kWh of energy. So if the meter advances by 0.45 cubic metres in an hour, that is about 5 kWh of gas, which costs roughly 37p at the capped rate.

Sense-check against your boiler

If your measured warm-up consumption is close to your boiler's full rated input, the boiler is running flat out and may be set hotter than it needs to be. Many combi boilers leave the factory with the radiator flow temperature set high, which pushes the boiler out of its efficient condensing mode. Turning the flow temperature down to around 60°C costs nothing and trials suggest it trims gas use by roughly 9% in a typical home. Our guide to boiler flow temperature shows exactly which dial to turn.

Central heating in a typical gas-heated three-bed home costs about 25p to 40p per hour to hold temperature and 60p to 75p for the first hour from cold, at the July to September 2026 capped gas rate of 7.33p per kWh. Flats run cheaper, large or poorly insulated houses dearer, and very cold weather pushes everything up because the boiler is replacing heat the house loses faster.

Because the hourly cost is just kilowatt hours times unit rate, every saving comes down to burning fewer kilowatt hours: a lower flow temperature, a thermostat setting one degree down, heating only the hours you are actually home, and insulation and draught-proofing to slow the leak you are paying to refill. Each of those levers gets its own guide in our heating section, and most of them cost little or nothing to pull.

Measure your own hour once in mild weather and once in cold, write the two numbers on a sticky note by the thermostat, and the daily question of whether to press the button stops being a mystery.

faq.q: How much gas does a boiler use per hour?
faq.a: For an average three-bed home, expect around 8 to 10 kWh of gas in the first hour while warming up from cold, falling to about 3 to 5 kWh per hour once the house is holding temperature. Small flats use roughly half that and large detached homes can use half as much again. The boiler's rated output, often 24 to 30 kW, is a maximum, not what it actually burns each hour.

faq.q: How much does it cost to run central heating for 8 hours?
faq.a: For a typical three-bed gas-heated home on a cold day, roughly £2.50 to £3.50 at the July to September 2026 capped rate of 7.33p per kWh: one expensive warm-up hour plus seven cheaper steady hours. A small well-insulated flat might manage the same eight hours for around £1.50, while a large draughty house could pass £5.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to heat one room with an electric heater than run the central heating?
faq.a: Only if the rest of the house genuinely stays cold. A 2 kW electric heater costs about 52p per hour at 26.11p per kWh, which is more than gas central heating typically costs to hold a whole average home at temperature. Electric heaters win for short stints in one small room, for example an hour in a home office, and lose as soon as you need warmth in more than one or two rooms.

faq.q: Does a bigger boiler cost more per hour to run?
faq.a: Not in itself. The boiler burns whatever the house demands, so a 30 kW boiler holding a warm house uses essentially the same gas as a 24 kW one in the same house. An oversized boiler can cost slightly more in practice because it cycles on and off more, which hurts efficiency, but the badge rating does not set your hourly cost.

faq.q: Why does my heating cost more per hour in January than in November?
faq.a: Your boiler replaces the heat the house loses, and heat loss rises with the temperature gap between inside and out. A house held at 20°C loses heat roughly twice as fast when it is 0°C outside as when it is 10°C, so the boiler burns roughly twice the gas per hour to keep up. The unit rate has not changed; the house is simply leaking more.
